{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun cluster in Kan'onji, Kagawa, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Ōnohara Kofun cluster | native_name =大野原古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Wankashizuka Kofun, entrance.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Wankashizuka Kofun entry | map_type = Japan Kagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ōnohara Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|05|16|N|133|39|48|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kan'onji, Kagawa]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Shikoku]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built =late 6th - mid-7th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|05|16|N|133|39|48|E|}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Ōnohara Kofun cluster'''|大野原古墳群|Ōnohara Kofun-gun|}} is a group of ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]]s located in the city of [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], on the island of [[Shikoku]] of [[Japan]]. The tumuli were collectively designated a National Historic Site in 2015 with the area under protection expanded in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003882|title=大野原古墳群|trans-title= Ōnohara Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== The Ōnohara Kofun cluster is located in the western part of Kagawa Prefecture, in the center of an [[alluvial fan]] formed by the Kushita River, overlooking the [[Seto Inland Sea]]. The site once consisted of over 170 tumuli, but most have been destroyed through urban encroachment and farmland improvement, and only the four largest tumuli have survived under National Historic Site protection. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been carried out since 2006. The Wankashizuka Kofun, Hirazuka Kofun and Iwakurazuka Kofun are large circular Kofuns ( {{nihongo|''{{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}), whereas the Kakuzuka Kofun is a large rectangular Kofun ({{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}). All have large stone [[burial chamber]]s measuring 10 or more meters in length. In particular, the Wankashizuka Kofun has a total length of 14.8 meters and floor area of 24.6 square meters, or 72.7 cubic meters, making it the largest in Shikoku. From artifacts and [[grave goods]] recovered, these tumuli are presumed to have been constructed from the late Kofun period from the end of the 6th century to the early 7th century. In the Shikoku region, there are many examples of individual tumuli of a class similar to the ones in this group, but the Ōnohara Kofun cluster is unique in that several large tumuli were built in a group over three generations for about 50 years.
The '''Wankashizuka Kofun''' is located behind the main hall of Onohara Hachiman Shrine. The tumulus is circular, measuring 37.2 meters in diameter and 9.5 meters in height, and was surrounded by a double moat, with a small embankment in between the moats. The burial chamber is a multi-room structure, consisting of a ear chamber, an antechamber, and a passageway, and opens to the southwest. It has a total length of 14.8 meters, with the rear chamber measuring 6.8 meters long, 3.6 meters widest, currently 3.9 meters high. Among the excavated items are pieces of [[Sue pottery]] pottery and fragments of a wooden coffin. These artifacts date the tumulus to the end of the 6th century.
The '''Hirazuka Kofun''' has a chapel of the Onohara Hachiman Shrine on its summit. The tumulus is circular, and with a diameter of 50.2 meters is the largest tumulus in Kagawa Prefecture. It is surrounded by an 8.4-meter wide moat. The burial chamber opens in the south-southwest direction and is a single-room structure with a total length of 13.2 meters. The rear chamber is 6.5 meters long, 3 meters wide at maximum, and 2.6 meters high and was found to contain Sure ware [[Haji ware]] and other items which date it to the beginning of the 7th century.
The '''Kakuzuka Kofun''' is a square tumulus located adjacent to Onohara Central Park. It measures 41.7 x 37.8 meters, making it then largest square tumuli in Kagawa Prefecture. It was surrounded by a moat with a width of approximately 3.3 meters. The burial chamber opened to the south-southeast and has a length of 12.5 meters, with an inner chamber measuring 4.7 meters long, 2.6 meters widest, and 2.4 meters high. The stone used was mostly granite. Inside, fragments of Sue pottery pottery were found, which were dated to around the first half of the 7th century
The '''Iwakurazuka Kofun''' is located in the precincts of Onohara Hachiman Shrine, close to the Wankarizuka Kofun, and excavations revealed that it was built over a portion of the outer moat of that tumulus. The Iwakurazuka Kofun is elliptical, with a major axis of 38.2 meters and minor axis of 36.4 meters and contained two burial chambers, both orientated to the east. Only the west chamber retains its stone walls. During excavation, late quantities of flat stones with single-character Buddhist votive inscriptions dating from the [[Edo Period]] were discovered.
The site is open to the public as an [[archaeological park]], but the interior of the burial chambers are not accessible.

[[Archaeological excavation]]s have been carried out since 2006. The Wankashizuka Kofun, Hirazuka Kofun and Iwakurazuka Kofun are large circular Kofuns ( {{nihongo|''{{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}), whereas the Kakuzuka Kofun is a large rectangular Kofun ({{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}). All have large stone [[burial chamber]]s measuring 10 or more meters in length. In particular, the Wankashizuka Kofun has a total length of 14.8 meters and floor area of 24.6 square meters, or 72.7 cubic meters, making it the largest in Shikoku. From artifacts and [[grave goods]] recovered, these tumuli are presumed to have been constructed from the late Kofun period from the end of the 6th century to the early 7th century. In the Shikoku region, there are many examples of individual tumuli of a class similar to the ones in this group, but the Ōnohara Kofun cluster is unique in that several large tumuli were built in a group over three generations for about 50 years.
The '''Wankashizuka Kofun''' is located behind the main hall of Onohara Hachiman Shrine. The tumulus is circular, measuring 37.2 meters in diameter and 9.5 meters in height, and was surrounded by a double moat, with a small embankment in between the moats. The burial chamber is a multi-room structure, consisting of a ear chamber, an antechamber, and a passageway, and opens to the southwest. It has a total length of 14.8 meters, with the rear chamber measuring 6.8 meters long, 3.6 meters widest, currently 3.9 meters high. Among the excavated items are pieces of [[Sue pottery]] pottery and fragments of a wooden coffin. These artifacts date the tumulus to the end of the 6th century.
The '''Hirazuka Kofun''' has a chapel of the Onohara Hachiman Shrine on its summit. The tumulus is circular, and with a diameter of 50.2 meters is the largest tumulus in Kagawa Prefecture. It is surrounded by an 8.4-meter wide moat. The burial chamber opens in the south-southwest direction and is a single-room structure with a total length of 13.2 meters. The rear chamber is 6.5 meters long, 3 meters wide at maximum, and 2.6 meters high and was found to contain Sure ware [[Haji pottery]] and other items which date it to the beginning of the 7th century.
The '''Kakuzuka Kofun''' is a square tumulus located adjacent to Onohara Central Park. It measures 41.7 x 37.8 meters, making it then largest square tumuli in Kagawa Prefecture. It was surrounded by a moat with a width of approximately 3.3 meters. The burial chamber opened to the south-southeast and has a length of 12.5 meters, with an inner chamber measuring 4.7 meters long, 2.6 meters widest, and 2.4 meters high. The stone used was mostly granite. Inside, fragments of Sue pottery pottery were found, which were dated to around the first half of the 7th century
The '''Iwakurazuka Kofun''' is located in the precincts of Onohara Hachiman Shrine, close to the Wankarizuka Kofun, and excavations revealed that it was built over a portion of the outer moat of that tumulus. The Iwakurazuka Kofun is elliptical, with a major axis of 38.2 meters and minor axis of 36.4 meters and contained two burial chambers, both orientated to the east. Only the west chamber retains its stone walls. During excavation, late quantities of flat stones with single-character Buddhist votive inscriptions dating from the [[Edo Period]] were discovered.
The site is open to the public as an [[archaeological park]], but the interior of the burial chambers are not accessible.

It was surrounded by a moat with a width of approximately 3.3 meters. Only the west chamber retains its stone walls. During excavation, late quantities of flat stones with single-character Buddhist votive inscriptions dating from the [[Edo Period]] were discovered.
The site is open to the public as an [[archaeological park]], but the interior of the burial chambers are not accessible.

{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun cluster in Kan'onji, Kagawa, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Ōnohara Kofun cluster | native_name =大野原古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Wankashizuka Kofun, entrance.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Wankashizuka Kofun entry | map_type = Japan Kagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ōnohara Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|05|16|N|133|39|48|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kan'onji, Kagawa]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Shikoku]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built =late 6th - mid-7th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|05|16|N|133|39|48|E|}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Ōnohara Kofun cluster'''|大野原古墳群|Ōnohara Kofun-gun|}} is a group of ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]]s located in the city of [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], on the island of [[Shikoku]] of [[Japan]]. The tumuli were collectively designated a National Historic Site in 2015 with the area under protection expanded in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003882|title=大野原古墳群|trans-title= Ōnohara Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== The Ōnohara Kofun cluster is located in the western part of Kagawa Prefecture, in the center of an [[alluvial fan]] formed by the Kushita River, overlooking the [[Seto Inland Sea]]. The site once consisted of over 170 tumuli, but most have been destroyed through urban encroachment and farmland improvement, and only the four largest tumuli have survived under National Historic Site protection. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been carried out since 2006. The Wankashizuka Kofun, Hirazuka Kofun and Iwakurazuka Kofun are large circular Kofuns ( {{nihongo|''{{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}), whereas the Kakuzuka Kofun is a large rectangular Kofun ({{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}). All have large stone [[burial chamber]]s measuring 10 or more meters in length. In particular, the Wankashizuka Kofun has a total length of 14.8 meters and floor area of 24.6 square meters, or 72.7 cubic meters, making it the largest in Shikoku. From artifacts and [[grave goods]] recovered, these tumuli are presumed to have been constructed from the late Kofun period from the end of the 6th century to the early 7th century. In the Shikoku region, there are many examples of individual tumuli of a class similar to the ones in this group, but the Ōnohara Kofun cluster is unique in that several large tumuli were built in a group over three generations for about 50 years.
The '''Wankashizuka Kofun''' is located behind the main hall of Onohara Hachiman Shrine. The tumulus is circular, measuring 37.2 meters in diameter and 9.5 meters in height, and was surrounded by a double moat, with a small embankment in between the moats. The burial chamber is a multi-room structure, consisting of a ear chamber, an antechamber, and a passageway, and opens to the southwest. It has a total length of 14.8 meters, with the rear chamber measuring 6.8 meters long, 3.6 meters widest, currently 3.9 meters high. Among the excavated items are pieces of [[Sue pottery]] pottery and fragments of a wooden coffin. These artifacts date the tumulus to the end of the 6th century.
The '''Hirazuka Kofun''' has a chapel of the Onohara Hachiman Shrine on its summit. The tumulus is circular, and with a diameter of 50.2 meters is the largest tumulus in Kagawa Prefecture. It is surrounded by an 8.4-meter wide moat. The burial chamber opens in the south-southwest direction and is a single-room structure with a total length of 13.2 meters. The rear chamber is 6.5 meters long, 3 meters wide at maximum, and 2.6 meters high and was found to contain Sure ware [[Haji pottery]] and other items which date it to the beginning of the 7th century.
The '''Kakuzuka Kofun''' is a square tumulus located adjacent to Onohara Central Park. It measures 41.7 x 37.8 meters, making it then largest square tumuli in Kagawa Prefecture. It was surrounded by a moat with a width of approximately 3.3 meters. The burial chamber opened to the south-southeast and has a length of 12.5 meters, with an inner chamber measuring 4.7 meters long, 2.6 meters widest, and 2.4 meters high. The stone used was mostly granite. Inside, fragments of Sue pottery pottery were found, which were dated to around the first half of the 7th century
The '''Iwakurazuka Kofun''' is located in the precincts of Onohara Hachiman Shrine, close to the Wankarizuka Kofun, and excavations revealed that it was built over a portion of the outer moat of that tumulus. The Iwakurazuka Kofun is elliptical, with a major axis of 38.2 meters and minor axis of 36.4 meters and contained two burial chambers, both orientated to the east. Only the west chamber retains its stone walls. During excavation, late quantities of flat stones with single-character Buddhist votive inscriptions dating from the [[Edo Period]] were discovered.
The site is open to the public as an [[archaeological park]], but the interior of the burial chambers are not accessible.

{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun cluster in Kan'onji, Kagawa, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Ōnohara Kofun cluster | native_name =大野原古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Wankashizuka Kofun, entrance.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Wankashizuka Kofun entry | map_type = Japan Kagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ōnohara Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|05|16|N|133|39|48|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kan'onji, Kagawa]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Shikoku]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built =late 6th - mid-7th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|05|16|N|133|39|48|E|}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Ōnohara Kofun cluster'''|大野原古墳群|Ōnohara Kofun-gun|}} is a group of ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]]s located in the city of [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], on the island of [[Shikoku]] of [[Japan]]. The tumuli were collectively designated a National Historic Site in 2015 with the area under protection expanded in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003882|title=大野原古墳群|trans-title= Ōnohara Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== The Ōnohara Kofun cluster is located in the western part of Kagawa Prefecture, in the center of an [[alluvial fan]] formed by the Kushita River, overlooking the [[Seto Inland Sea]]. The site once consisted of over 170 tumuli, but most have been destroyed through urban encroachment and farmland improvement, and only the four largest tumuli have survived under National Historic Site protection. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been carried out since 2006. The Wankashizuka Kofun, Hirazuka Kofun and Iwakurazuka Kofun are large circular Kofuns ( {{nihongo|''{{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}), whereas the Kakuzuka Kofun is a large rectangular Kofun ({{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}). All have large stone [[burial chamber]]s measuring 10 or more meters in length. In particular, the Wankashizuka Kofun has a total length of 14.8 meters and floor area of 24.6 square meters, or 72.7 cubic meters, making it the largest in Shikoku. From artifacts and [[grave goods]] recovered, these tumuli are presumed to have been constructed from the late Kofun period from the end of the 6th century to the early 7th century. In the Shikoku region, there are many examples of individual tumuli of a class similar to the ones in this group, but the Ōnohara Kofun cluster is unique in that several large tumuli were built in a group over three generations for about 50 years.
The '''Wankashizuka Kofun''' is located behind the main hall of Onohara Hachiman Shrine. The tumulus is circular, measuring 37.2 meters in diameter and 9.5 meters in height, and was surrounded by a double moat, with a small embankment in between the moats. The burial chamber is a multi-room structure, consisting of a ear chamber, an antechamber, and a passageway, and opens to the southwest. It has a total length of 14.8 meters, with the rear chamber measuring 6.8 meters long, 3.6 meters widest, currently 3.9 meters high. Among the excavated items are pieces of [[Sue pottery]] pottery and fragments of a wooden coffin. These artifacts date the tumulus to the end of the 6th century.
The '''Hirazuka Kofun''' has a chapel of the Onohara Hachiman Shrine on its summit. The tumulus is circular, and with a diameter of 50.2 meters is the largest tumulus in Kagawa Prefecture. It is surrounded by an 8.4-meter wide moat. The burial chamber opens in the south-southwest direction and is a single-room structure with a total length of 13.2 meters. The rear chamber is 6.5 meters long, 3 meters wide at maximum, and 2.6 meters high and was found to contain Sure ware [[Haji pottery]] and other items which date it to the beginning of the 7th century.
The '''Kakuzuka Kofun''' is a square tumulus located adjacent to Onohara Central Park. It measures 41.7 x 37.8 meters, making it then largest square tumuli in Kagawa Prefecture. It was surrounded by a moat with a width of approximately 3.3 meters. The burial chamber opened to the south-southeast and has a length of 12.5 meters, with an inner chamber measuring 4.7 meters long, 2.6 meters widest, and 2.4 meters high. The stone used was mostly granite. Inside, fragments of Sue pottery pottery were found, which were dated to around the first half of the 7th century
The '''Iwakurazuka Kofun''' is located in the precincts of Onohara Hachiman Shrine, close to the Wankarizuka Kofun, and excavations revealed that it was built over a portion of the outer moat of that tumulus. The Iwakurazuka Kofun is elliptical, with a major axis of 38.2 meters and minor axis of 36.4 meters and contained two burial chambers, both orientated to the east. Only the west chamber retains its stone walls. During excavation, late quantities of flat stones with single-character Buddhist votive inscriptions dating from the [[Edo Period]] were discovered.
The site is open to the public as an [[archaeological park]], but the interior of the burial chambers are not accessible.
{| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center;background:#ffffff;white-space:nowrap;font-size:85%" |+ Ōnohara Kofun cluster !name !location !type !Size !Date |- |{{nihongo|'''Wankashizuka Kofun'''|椀貸塚古墳|}} ||{{coords|34|5|15.75|N|133|39|47.65|E|type:landmark|}}||circular||diameter 37.2m dia x 9.5m high; burial chamber: 14.8m||late 6th C |- |{{nihongo|'''Hirazuka Kofun'''|平塚古墳|}} ||{{coords|34|5|1.08|N|133|39|34.8|E|type:landmark|}}||circular||dia 50.2 m dia x 7m high||early 7th C |- |{{nihongo|'''Kakuzuka Kofun'''|角塚古墳|}}||{{coords|34|5|5.85|N|133|39|38.3|E|type:landmark|}}||square||41.7m x 37.8m x 9 m high||early 7th C |- |{{nihongo|'''Iwakurazuka Kofun'''|岩倉塚古墳|}}||{{coords|34|5|16.18|N|133|39|48.87|E|type:landmark|}}||circular||36.4-38.2m dia||late 6th C |}

The tumuli were collectively designated a National Historic Site in 2015 with the area under protection expanded in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003882|title=大野原古墳群|trans-title= Ōnohara Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== The Ōnohara Kofun cluster is located in the western part of Kagawa Prefecture, in the center of an [[alluvial fan]] formed by the Kushita River, overlooking the [[Seto Inland Sea]]. The site once consisted of over 170 tumuli, but most have been destroyed through urban encroachment and farmland improvement, and only the four largest tumuli have survived under National Historic Site protection. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been carried out since 2006. The Wankashizuka Kofun, Hirazuka Kofun and Iwakurazuka Kofun are large circular Kofuns ( {{nihongo|''{{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}), whereas the Kakuzuka Kofun is a large rectangular Kofun ({{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}). All have large stone [[burial chamber]]s measuring 10 or more meters in length. In particular, the Wankashizuka Kofun has a total length of 14.8 meters and floor area of 24.6 square meters, or 72.7 cubic meters, making it the largest in Shikoku. From artifacts and [[grave goods]] recovered, these tumuli are presumed to have been constructed from the late Kofun period from the end of the 6th century to the early 7th century. In the Shikoku region, there are many examples of individual tumuli of a class similar to the ones in this group, but the Ōnohara Kofun cluster is unique in that several large tumuli were built in a group over three generations for about 50 years.
The '''Wankashizuka Kofun''' is located behind the main hall of Onohara Hachiman Shrine. The tumulus is circular, measuring 37.2 meters in diameter and 9.5 meters in height, and was surrounded by a double moat, with a small embankment in between the moats. The burial chamber is a multi-room structure, consisting of a ear chamber, an antechamber, and a passageway, and opens to the southwest. It has a total length of 14.8 meters, with the rear chamber measuring 6.8 meters long, 3.6 meters widest, currently 3.9 meters high. Among the excavated items are pieces of [[Sue pottery]] pottery and fragments of a wooden coffin. These artifacts date the tumulus to the end of the 6th century.
The '''Hirazuka Kofun''' has a chapel of the Onohara Hachiman Shrine on its summit. The tumulus is circular, and with a diameter of 50.2 meters is the largest tumulus in Kagawa Prefecture. It is surrounded by an 8.4-meter wide moat. The burial chamber opens in the south-southwest direction and is a single-room structure with a total length of 13.2 meters. The rear chamber is 6.5 meters long, 3 meters wide at maximum, and 2.6 meters high and was found to contain Sure ware [[Haji pottery]] and other items which date it to the beginning of the 7th century.
The '''Kakuzuka Kofun''' is a square tumulus located adjacent to Onohara Central Park. It measures 41.7 x 37.8 meters, making it then largest square tumuli in Kagawa Prefecture. It was surrounded by a moat with a width of approximately 3.3 meters. The burial chamber opened to the south-southeast and has a length of 12.5 meters, with an inner chamber measuring 4.7 meters long, 2.6 meters widest, and 2.4 meters high. The stone used was mostly granite. Inside, fragments of Sue pottery pottery were found, which were dated to around the first half of the 7th century
The '''Iwakurazuka Kofun''' is located in the precincts of Onohara Hachiman Shrine, close to the Wankarizuka Kofun, and excavations revealed that it was built over a portion of the outer moat of that tumulus. The Iwakurazuka Kofun is elliptical, with a major axis of 38.2 meters and minor axis of 36.4 meters and contained two burial chambers, both orientated to the east. Only the west chamber retains its stone walls. During excavation, late quantities of flat stones with single-character Buddhist votive inscriptions dating from the [[Edo Period]] were discovered.
The site is open to the public as an [[archaeological park]], but the interior of the burial chambers are not accessible.

The {{nihongo|'''Ōnohara Kofun cluster'''|大野原古墳群|Ōnohara Kofun-gun|}} is a group of ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]]s located in the city of [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], on the island of [[Shikoku]] of [[Japan]]. {{Short description|Kofun period Kofun cluster in Kan'onji, Kagawa, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Ōnohara Kofun cluster | native_name =大野原古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Wankashizuka Kofun, entrance.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Wankashizuka Kofun entry | map_type = Japan Kagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ōnohara Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|05|16|N|133|39|48|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kan'onji, Kagawa]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Shikoku]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built =late 6th - mid-7th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|05|16|N|133|39|48|E|}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Ōnohara Kofun cluster'''|大野原古墳群|Ōnohara Kofun-gun|}} is a group of ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]]s located in the city of [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], on the island of [[Shikoku]] of [[Japan]]. The tumuli were collectively designated a National Historic Site in 2015 with the area under protection expanded in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003882|title=大野原古墳群|trans-title= Ōnohara Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== The Ōnohara Kofun cluster is located in the western part of Kagawa Prefecture, in the center of an [[alluvial fan]] formed by the Kushita River, overlooking the [[Seto Inland Sea]]. The site once consisted of over 170 tumuli, but most have been destroyed through urban encroachment and farmland improvement, and only the four largest tumuli have survived under National Historic Site protection. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been carried out since 2006. The Wankashizuka Kofun, Hirazuka Kofun and Iwakurazuka Kofun are large circular Kofuns ( {{nihongo|''{{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}), whereas the Kakuzuka Kofun is a large rectangular Kofun ({{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}). All have large stone [[burial chamber]]s measuring 10 or more meters in length. In particular, the Wankashizuka Kofun has a total length of 14.8 meters and floor area of 24.6 square meters, or 72.7 cubic meters, making it the largest in Shikoku. From artifacts and [[grave goods]] recovered, these tumuli are presumed to have been constructed from the late Kofun period from the end of the 6th century to the early 7th century. In the Shikoku region, there are many examples of individual tumuli of a class similar to the ones in this group, but the Ōnohara Kofun cluster is unique in that several large tumuli were built in a group over three generations for about 50 years.
The '''Wankashizuka Kofun''' is located behind the main hall of Onohara Hachiman Shrine. The tumulus is circular, measuring 37.2 meters in diameter and 9.5 meters in height, and was surrounded by a double moat, with a small embankment in between the moats. The burial chamber is a multi-room structure, consisting of a ear chamber, an antechamber, and a passageway, and opens to the southwest. It has a total length of 14.8 meters, with the rear chamber measuring 6.8 meters long, 3.6 meters widest, currently 3.9 meters high. Among the excavated items are pieces of [[Sue pottery]] pottery and fragments of a wooden coffin. These artifacts date the tumulus to the end of the 6th century.
The '''Hirazuka Kofun''' has a chapel of the Onohara Hachiman Shrine on its summit. The tumulus is circular, and with a diameter of 50.2 meters is the largest tumulus in Kagawa Prefecture. It is surrounded by an 8.4-meter wide moat. The burial chamber opens in the south-southwest direction and is a single-room structure with a total length of 13.2 meters. The rear chamber is 6.5 meters long, 3 meters wide at maximum, and 2.6 meters high and was found to contain Sure ware [[Haji pottery]] and other items which date it to the beginning of the 7th century.
The '''Kakuzuka Kofun''' is a square tumulus located adjacent to Onohara Central Park. It measures 41.7 x 37.8 meters, making it then largest square tumuli in Kagawa Prefecture. It was surrounded by a moat with a width of approximately 3.3 meters. The burial chamber opened to the south-southeast and has a length of 12.5 meters, with an inner chamber measuring 4.7 meters long, 2.6 meters widest, and 2.4 meters high. The stone used was mostly granite. Inside, fragments of Sue pottery pottery were found, which were dated to around the first half of the 7th century
The '''Iwakurazuka Kofun''' is located in the precincts of Onohara Hachiman Shrine, close to the Wankarizuka Kofun, and excavations revealed that it was built over a portion of the outer moat of that tumulus. The Iwakurazuka Kofun is elliptical, with a major axis of 38.2 meters and minor axis of 36.4 meters and contained two burial chambers, both orientated to the east. Only the west chamber retains its stone walls. During excavation, late quantities of flat stones with single-character Buddhist votive inscriptions dating from the [[Edo Period]] were discovered.
The site is open to the public as an [[archaeological park]], but the interior of the burial chambers are not accessible.
{| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center;background:#ffffff;white-space:nowrap;font-size:85%" |+ Ōnohara Kofun cluster !name !location !type !Size !Date |- |{{nihongo|'''Wankashizuka Kofun'''|椀貸塚古墳|}} ||{{coords|34|5|15.75|N|133|39|47.65|E|type:landmark|}}||circular||diameter 37.2m dia x 9.5m high; burial chamber: 14.8m||late 6th C |- |{{nihongo|'''Hirazuka Kofun'''|平塚古墳|}} ||{{coords|34|5|1.08|N|133|39|34.8|E|type:landmark|}}||circular||dia 50.2 m dia x 7m high||early 7th C |- |{{nihongo|'''Kakuzuka Kofun'''|角塚古墳|}}||{{coords|34|5|5.85|N|133|39|38.3|E|type:landmark|}}||square||41.7m x 37.8m x 9 m high||early 7th C |- |{{nihongo|'''Iwakurazuka Kofun'''|岩倉塚古墳|}}||{{coords|34|5|16.18|N|133|39|48.87|E|type:landmark|}}||circular||36.4-38.2m dia||late 6th C |}
==Gallery== <gallery> Wankashizuka Kofun, funkyu-3.jpg| Wankashizuka Kofun Hirazuka Kofun (Kan-onji), entrance-1.jpg|Hirazuka Kofun Kakuzuka Kofun (Kan-onji), entrance-2.jpg|Kakuzuka Kofun Iwakurazuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|Iwakurazuka Kofun </gallery>

The '''Iwakurazuka Kofun''' is located in the precincts of Onohara Hachiman Shrine, close to the Wankarizuka Kofun, and excavations revealed that it was built over a portion of the outer moat of that tumulus. The Iwakurazuka Kofun is elliptical, with a major axis of 38.2 meters and minor axis of 36.4 meters and contained two burial chambers, both orientated to the east. Only the west chamber retains its stone walls. During excavation, late quantities of flat stones with single-character Buddhist votive inscriptions dating from the [[Edo Period]] were discovered.
The site is open to the public as an [[archaeological park]], but the interior of the burial chambers are not accessible.

{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun cluster in Kan'onji, Kagawa, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Ōnohara Kofun cluster | native_name =大野原古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Wankashizuka Kofun, entrance.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Wankashizuka Kofun entry | map_type = Japan Kagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ōnohara Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|05|16|N|133|39|48|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kan'onji, Kagawa]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Shikoku]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built =late 6th - mid-7th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|05|16|N|133|39|48|E|}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Ōnohara Kofun cluster'''|大野原古墳群|Ōnohara Kofun-gun|}} is a group of ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]]s located in the city of [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], on the island of [[Shikoku]] of [[Japan]]. The tumuli were collectively designated a National Historic Site in 2015 with the area under protection expanded in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003882|title=大野原古墳群|trans-title= Ōnohara Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== The Ōnohara Kofun cluster is located in the western part of Kagawa Prefecture, in the center of an [[alluvial fan]] formed by the Kushita River, overlooking the [[Seto Inland Sea]]. The site once consisted of over 170 tumuli, but most have been destroyed through urban encroachment and farmland improvement, and only the four largest tumuli have survived under National Historic Site protection. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been carried out since 2006. The Wankashizuka Kofun, Hirazuka Kofun and Iwakurazuka Kofun are large circular Kofuns ( {{nihongo|''{{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}), whereas the Kakuzuka Kofun is a large rectangular Kofun ({{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=draft}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}). All have large stone [[burial chamber]]s measuring 10 or more meters in length. In particular, the Wankashizuka Kofun has a total length of 14.8 meters and floor area of 24.6 square meters, or 72.7 cubic meters, making it the largest in Shikoku. From artifacts and [[grave goods]] recovered, these tumuli are presumed to have been constructed from the late Kofun period from the end of the 6th century to the early 7th century. In the Shikoku region, there are many examples of individual tumuli of a class similar to the ones in this group, but the Ōnohara Kofun cluster is unique in that several large tumuli were built in a group over three generations for about 50 years.
The '''Wankashizuka Kofun''' is located behind the main hall of Onohara Hachiman Shrine. The tumulus is circular, measuring 37.2 meters in diameter and 9.5 meters in height, and was surrounded by a double moat, with a small embankment in between the moats. The burial chamber is a multi-room structure, consisting of a ear chamber, an antechamber, and a passageway, and opens to the southwest. It has a total length of 14.8 meters, with the rear chamber measuring 6.8 meters long, 3.6 meters widest, currently 3.9 meters high. Among the excavated items are pieces of [[Sue pottery]] pottery and fragments of a wooden coffin. These artifacts date the tumulus to the end of the 6th century.
The '''Hirazuka Kofun''' has a chapel of the Onohara Hachiman Shrine on its summit. The tumulus is circular, and with a diameter of 50.2 meters is the largest tumulus in Kagawa Prefecture. It is surrounded by an 8.4-meter wide moat. The burial chamber opens in the south-southwest direction and is a single-room structure with a total length of 13.2 meters. The rear chamber is 6.5 meters long, 3 meters wide at maximum, and 2.6 meters high and was found to contain Sure ware [[Haji pottery]] and other items which date it to the beginning of the 7th century.
The '''Kakuzuka Kofun''' is a square tumulus located adjacent to Onohara Central Park. It measures 41.7 x 37.8 meters, making it then largest square tumuli in Kagawa Prefecture. It was surrounded by a moat with a width of approximately 3.3 meters. The burial chamber opened to the south-southeast and has a length of 12.5 meters, with an inner chamber measuring 4.7 meters long, 2.6 meters widest, and 2.4 meters high. The stone used was mostly granite. Inside, fragments of Sue pottery pottery were found, which were dated to around the first half of the 7th century
The '''Iwakurazuka Kofun''' is located in the precincts of Onohara Hachiman Shrine, close to the Wankarizuka Kofun, and excavations revealed that it was built over a portion of the outer moat of that tumulus. The Iwakurazuka Kofun is elliptical, with a major axis of 38.2 meters and minor axis of 36.4 meters and contained two burial chambers, both orientated to the east. Only the west chamber retains its stone walls. During excavation, late quantities of flat stones with single-character Buddhist votive inscriptions dating from the [[Edo Period]] were discovered.
The site is open to the public as an [[archaeological park]], but the interior of the burial chambers are not accessible.

{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun cluster in Kan'onji, Kagawa, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Ōnohara Kofun cluster | native_name =大野原古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Wankashizuka Kofun, entrance.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Wankashizuka Kofun entry | map_type = Japan Kagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ōnohara Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|05|16|N|133|39|48|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kan'onji, Kagawa]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Shikoku]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built =late 6th - mid-7th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|05|16|N|133|39|48|E|}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Ōnohara Kofun cluster'''|大野原古墳群|Ōnohara Kofun-gun|}} is a group of ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]]s located in the city of [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], on the island of [[Shikoku]] of [[Japan]]. The tumuli were collectively designated a National Historic Site in 2015 with the area under protection expanded in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003882|title=大野原古墳群|trans-title= Ōnohara Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== The Ōnohara Kofun cluster is located in the western part of Kagawa Prefecture, in the center of an [[alluvial fan]] formed by the Kushita River, overlooking the [[Seto Inland Sea]]. The site once consisted of over 170 tumuli, but most have been destroyed through urban encroachment and farmland improvement, and only the four largest tumuli have survived under National Historic Site protection. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been carried out since 2006. The Wankashizuka Kofun, Hirazuka Kofun and Iwakurazuka Kofun are large circular Kofuns ( {{nihongo|''{{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}), whereas the Kakuzuka Kofun is a large rectangular Kofun ({{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=draft}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}). All have large stone [[burial chamber]]s measuring 10 or more meters in length. In particular, the Wankashizuka Kofun has a total length of 14.8 meters and floor area of 24.6 square meters, or 72.7 cubic meters, making it the largest in Shikoku. From artifacts and [[grave goods]] recovered, these tumuli are presumed to have been constructed from the late Kofun period from the end of the 6th century to the early 7th century. In the Shikoku region, there are many examples of individual tumuli of a class similar to the ones in this group, but the Ōnohara Kofun cluster is unique in that several large tumuli were built in a group over three generations for about 50 years.
The '''Wankashizuka Kofun''' is located behind the main hall of Onohara Hachiman Shrine. The tumulus is circular, measuring 37.2 meters in diameter and 9.5 meters in height, and was surrounded by a double moat, with a small embankment in between the moats. The burial chamber is a multi-room structure, consisting of a ear chamber, an antechamber, and a passageway, and opens to the southwest. It has a total length of 14.8 meters, with the rear chamber measuring 6.8 meters long, 3.6 meters widest, currently 3.9 meters high. Among the excavated items are pieces of [[Sue pottery]] pottery and fragments of a wooden coffin. These artifacts date the tumulus to the end of the 6th century.
The '''Hirazuka Kofun''' has a chapel of the Onohara Hachiman Shrine on its summit. The tumulus is circular, and with a diameter of 50.2 meters is the largest tumulus in Kagawa Prefecture. It is surrounded by an 8.4-meter wide moat. The burial chamber opens in the south-southwest direction and is a single-room structure with a total length of 13.2 meters. The rear chamber is 6.5 meters long, 3 meters wide at maximum, and 2.6 meters high and was found to contain Sure ware [[Haji pottery]] and other items which date it to the beginning of the 7th century.
The '''Kakuzuka Kofun''' is a square tumulus located adjacent to Onohara Central Park. It measures 41.7 x 37.8 meters, making it then largest square tumuli in Kagawa Prefecture. It was surrounded by a moat with a width of approximately 3.3 meters. The burial chamber opened to the south-southeast and has a length of 12.5 meters, with an inner chamber measuring 4.7 meters long, 2.6 meters widest, and 2.4 meters high. The stone used was mostly granite. Inside, fragments of Sue pottery pottery were found, which were dated to around the first half of the 7th century
The '''Iwakurazuka Kofun''' is located in the precincts of Onohara Hachiman Shrine, close to the Wankarizuka Kofun, and excavations revealed that it was built over a portion of the outer moat of that tumulus. The Iwakurazuka Kofun is elliptical, with a major axis of 38.2 meters and minor axis of 36.4 meters and contained two burial chambers, both orientated to the east. Only the west chamber retains its stone walls. During excavation, late quantities of flat stones with single-character Buddhist votive inscriptions dating from the [[Edo Period]] were discovered.
The site is open to the public as an [[archaeological park]], but the interior of the burial chambers are not accessible.

revision.text:
{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun cluster in Kan'onji, Kagawa, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Ōnohara Kofun cluster | native_name =大野原古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Wankashizuka Kofun, entrance.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Wankashizuka Kofun entry | map_type = Japan Kagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ōnohara Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|05|16|N|133|39|48|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kan'onji, Kagawa]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Shikoku]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built =late 6th - mid-7th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|05|16|N|133|39|48|E|}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Ōnohara Kofun cluster'''|大野原古墳群|Ōnohara Kofun-gun|}} is a group of ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]]s located in the city of [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], on the island of [[Shikoku]] of [[Japan]]. The tumuli were collectively designated a National Historic Site in 2015 with the area under protection expanded in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003882|title=大野原古墳群|trans-title= Ōnohara Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== The Ōnohara Kofun cluster is located in the western part of Kagawa Prefecture, in the center of an [[alluvial fan]] formed by the Kushita River, overlooking the [[Seto Inland Sea]]. The site once consisted of over 170 tumuli, but most have been destroyed through urban encroachment and farmland improvement, and only the four largest tumuli have survived under National Historic Site protection. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been carried out since 2006. The Wankashizuka Kofun, Hirazuka Kofun and Iwakurazuka Kofun are large circular Kofuns ( {{nihongo|''{{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}), whereas the Kakuzuka Kofun is a large rectangular Kofun ({{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=draft}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}). All have large stone [[burial chamber]]s measuring 10 or more meters in length. In particular, the Wankashizuka Kofun has a total length of 14.8 meters and floor area of 24.6 square meters, or 72.7 cubic meters, making it the largest in Shikoku. From artifacts and [[grave goods]] recovered, these tumuli are presumed to have been constructed from the late Kofun period from the end of the 6th century to the early 7th century. In the Shikoku region, there are many examples of individual tumuli of a class similar to the ones in this group, but the Ōnohara Kofun cluster is unique in that several large tumuli were built in a group over three generations for about 50 years.
The '''Wankashizuka Kofun''' is located behind the main hall of Onohara Hachiman Shrine. The tumulus is circular, measuring 37.2 meters in diameter and 9.5 meters in height, and was surrounded by a double moat, with a small embankment in between the moats. The burial chamber is a multi-room structure, consisting of a ear chamber, an antechamber, and a passageway, and opens to the southwest. It has a total length of 14.8 meters, with the rear chamber measuring 6.8 meters long, 3.6 meters widest, currently 3.9 meters high. Among the excavated items are pieces of [[Sue pottery]] pottery and fragments of a wooden coffin. These artifacts date the tumulus to the end of the 6th century.
The '''Hirazuka Kofun''' has a chapel of the Onohara Hachiman Shrine on its summit. The tumulus is circular, and with a diameter of 50.2 meters is the largest tumulus in Kagawa Prefecture. It is surrounded by an 8.4-meter wide moat. The burial chamber opens in the south-southwest direction and is a single-room structure with a total length of 13.2 meters. The rear chamber is 6.5 meters long, 3 meters wide at maximum, and 2.6 meters high and was found to contain Sure ware [[Haji pottery]] and other items which date it to the beginning of the 7th century.
The '''Kakuzuka Kofun''' is a square tumulus located adjacent to Onohara Central Park. It measures 41.7 x 37.8 meters, making it then largest square tumuli in Kagawa Prefecture. It was surrounded by a moat with a width of approximately 3.3 meters. The burial chamber opened to the south-southeast and has a length of 12.5 meters, with an inner chamber measuring 4.7 meters long, 2.6 meters widest, and 2.4 meters high. The stone used was mostly granite. Inside, fragments of Sue pottery pottery were found, which were dated to around the first half of the 7th century
The '''Iwakurazuka Kofun''' is located in the precincts of Onohara Hachiman Shrine, close to the Wankarizuka Kofun, and excavations revealed that it was built over a portion of the outer moat of that tumulus. The Iwakurazuka Kofun is elliptical, with a major axis of 38.2 meters and minor axis of 36.4 meters and contained two burial chambers, both orientated to the east. Only the west chamber retains its stone walls. During excavation, late quantities of flat stones with single-character Buddhist votive inscriptions dating from the [[Edo Period]] were discovered.
The site is open to the public as an [[archaeological park]], but the interior of the burial chambers are not accessible.
{| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center;background:#ffffff;white-space:nowrap;font-size:85%" |+ Ōnohara Kofun cluster !name !location !type !Size !Date |- |{{nihongo|'''Wankashizuka Kofun'''|椀貸塚古墳|}} ||{{coords|34|5|15.75|N|133|39|47.65|E|type:landmark|}}||circular||diameter 37.2m dia x 9.5m high; burial chamber: 14.8m||late 6th C |- |{{nihongo|'''Hirazuka Kofun'''|平塚古墳|}} ||{{coords|34|5|1.08|N|133|39|34.8|E|type:landmark|}}||circular||dia 50.2 m dia x 7m high||early 7th C |- |{{nihongo|'''Kakuzuka Kofun'''|角塚古墳|}}||{{coords|34|5|5.85|N|133|39|38.3|E|type:landmark|}}||square||41.7m x 37.8m x 9 m high||early 7th C |- |{{nihongo|'''Iwakurazuka Kofun'''|岩倉塚古墳|}}||{{coords|34|5|16.18|N|133|39|48.87|E|type:landmark|}}||circular||36.4-38.2m dia||late 6th C |}
==Gallery== <gallery> Wankashizuka Kofun, funkyu-3.jpg| Wankashizuka Kofun Hirazuka Kofun (Kan-onji), entrance-1.jpg|Hirazuka Kofun Kakuzuka Kofun (Kan-onji), entrance-2.jpg|Kakuzuka Kofun Iwakurazuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|Iwakurazuka Kofun </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kagawa)]]

==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:-inline|Onohara Kofun Group (Kan'onji)}} *[https://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/30/29644.html Kan'onji City home page] {{in lang|ja}} [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Ōnohara Kofun cluster]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Ōnohara Kofun cluster]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōnohara Kofun cluster]][[Category:qq]] {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kan'onji, Kagawa]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 3]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:Ōnohara Kofun cluster]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:方墳]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:completion tier 4]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Ōnohara Kofun cluster]] [[en:Ōnohara Kofun cluster]]

[[Archaeological excavation]]s have been carried out since 2006. The Wankashizuka Kofun, Hirazuka Kofun and Iwakurazuka Kofun are large circular Kofuns ( {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}), whereas the Kakuzuka Kofun is a large rectangular Kofun ({{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}). All have large stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s measuring 10 or more meters in length. In particular, the Wankashizuka Kofun has a total length of 14.8 meters and floor area of 24.6 square meters, or 72.7 cubic meters, making it the largest in Shikoku. From artifacts and [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] recovered, these tumuli are presumed to have been constructed from the late Kofun period from the end of the 6th century to the early 7th century. In the Shikoku region, there are many examples of individual tumuli of a class similar to the ones in this group, but the Ōnohara Kofun cluster is unique in that several large tumuli were built in a group over three generations for about 50 years.
The '''Wankashizuka Kofun''' is located behind the main hall of Onohara Hachiman Shrine. The tumulus is circular, measuring 37.2 meters in diameter and 9.5 meters in height, and was surrounded by a double moat, with a small embankment in between the moats. The burial chamber is a multi-room structure, consisting of a ear chamber, an antechamber, and a passageway, and opens to the southwest. It has a total length of 14.8 meters, with the rear chamber measuring 6.8 meters long, 3.6 meters widest, currently 3.9 meters high. Among the excavated items are pieces of [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery and fragments of a wooden coffin. These artifacts date the tumulus to the end of the 6th century.
The '''Hirazuka Kofun''' has a chapel of the Onohara Hachiman Shrine on its summit. The tumulus is circular, and with a diameter of 50.2 meters is the largest tumulus in Kagawa Prefecture. It is surrounded by an 8.4-meter wide moat. The burial chamber opens in the south-southwest direction and is a single-room structure with a total length of 13.2 meters. The rear chamber is 6.5 meters long, 3 meters wide at maximum, and 2.6 meters high and was found to contain Sure ware [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and other items which date it to the beginning of the 7th century.
The '''Kakuzuka Kofun''' is a square tumulus located adjacent to Onohara Central Park. It measures 41.7 x 37.8 meters, making it then largest square tumuli in Kagawa Prefecture. It was surrounded by a moat with a width of approximately 3.3 meters. The burial chamber opened to the south-southeast and has a length of 12.5 meters, with an inner chamber measuring 4.7 meters long, 2.6 meters widest, and 2.4 meters high. The stone used was mostly granite. Inside, fragments of Sue pottery pottery were found, which were dated to around the first half of the 7th century
The '''Iwakurazuka Kofun''' is located in the precincts of Onohara Hachiman Shrine, close to the Wankarizuka Kofun, and excavations revealed that it was built over a portion of the outer moat of that tumulus. The Iwakurazuka Kofun is elliptical, with a major axis of 38.2 meters and minor axis of 36.4 meters and contained two burial chambers, both orientated to the east. Only the west chamber retains its stone walls. During excavation, late quantities of flat stones with single-character Buddhist votive inscriptions dating from the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] were discovered.
The site is open to the public as an [[:en:Archaeological park|Archaeological park]], but the interior of the burial chambers are not accessible.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Onohara Kofun Group (Kan'onji)]][[en:Ōnohara Kofun cluster]][[ja:大野原古墳群 (観音寺市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q105070840]] Wikidata name: Onohara Kofun Group (Kan'onji) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Kofun period Kofun cluster in Kan'onji, Kagawa, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Ōnohara Kofun cluster | native_name =大野原古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Wankashizuka Kofun, entrance.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Wankashizuka Kofun entry | map_type = Japan Kagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ōnohara Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|05|16|N|133|39|48|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location =[[:en:Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji, Kagawa]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]; {{ill|Kan'onji|WD=Q861270}}| region = [[Shikoku]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built =late 6th - mid-7th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|05|16|N|133|39|48|E|}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Ōnohara Kofun cluster'''|大野原古墳群|Ōnohara Kofun-gun|}} is a group of ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]]s located in the city of [[:en:Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]], on the island of [[Shikoku]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumuli were collectively designated a National Historic Site in 2015 with the area under protection expanded in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003882|title=大野原古墳群|trans-title= Ōnohara Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== The Ōnohara Kofun cluster is located in the western part of Kagawa Prefecture, in the center of an [[:en:Alluvial fan|Alluvial fan]] formed by the Kushita River, overlooking the [[Seto Inland Sea]]. The site once consisted of over 170 tumuli, but most have been destroyed through urban encroachment and farmland improvement, and only the four largest tumuli have survived under National Historic Site protection. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been carried out since 2006. The Wankashizuka Kofun, Hirazuka Kofun and Iwakurazuka Kofun are large circular Kofuns ( {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}), whereas the Kakuzuka Kofun is a large rectangular Kofun ({{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}). All have large stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s measuring 10 or more meters in length. In particular, the Wankashizuka Kofun has a total length of 14.8 meters and floor area of 24.6 square meters, or 72.7 cubic meters, making it the largest in Shikoku. From artifacts and [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] recovered, these tumuli are presumed to have been constructed from the late Kofun period from the end of the 6th century to the early 7th century. In the Shikoku region, there are many examples of individual tumuli of a class similar to the ones in this group, but the Ōnohara Kofun cluster is unique in that several large tumuli were built in a group over three generations for about 50 years.
The '''Wankashizuka Kofun''' is located behind the main hall of Onohara Hachiman Shrine. The tumulus is circular, measuring 37.2 meters in diameter and 9.5 meters in height, and was surrounded by a double moat, with a small embankment in between the moats. The burial chamber is a multi-room structure, consisting of a ear chamber, an antechamber, and a passageway, and opens to the southwest. It has a total length of 14.8 meters, with the rear chamber measuring 6.8 meters long, 3.6 meters widest, currently 3.9 meters high. Among the excavated items are pieces of [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery and fragments of a wooden coffin. These artifacts date the tumulus to the end of the 6th century.
The '''Hirazuka Kofun''' has a chapel of the Onohara Hachiman Shrine on its summit. The tumulus is circular, and with a diameter of 50.2 meters is the largest tumulus in Kagawa Prefecture. It is surrounded by an 8.4-meter wide moat. The burial chamber opens in the south-southwest direction and is a single-room structure with a total length of 13.2 meters. The rear chamber is 6.5 meters long, 3 meters wide at maximum, and 2.6 meters high and was found to contain Sure ware [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and other items which date it to the beginning of the 7th century.
The '''Kakuzuka Kofun''' is a square tumulus located adjacent to Onohara Central Park. It measures 41.7 x 37.8 meters, making it then largest square tumuli in Kagawa Prefecture. It was surrounded by a moat with a width of approximately 3.3 meters. The burial chamber opened to the south-southeast and has a length of 12.5 meters, with an inner chamber measuring 4.7 meters long, 2.6 meters widest, and 2.4 meters high. The stone used was mostly granite. Inside, fragments of Sue pottery pottery were found, which were dated to around the first half of the 7th century
The '''Iwakurazuka Kofun''' is located in the precincts of Onohara Hachiman Shrine, close to the Wankarizuka Kofun, and excavations revealed that it was built over a portion of the outer moat of that tumulus. The Iwakurazuka Kofun is elliptical, with a major axis of 38.2 meters and minor axis of 36.4 meters and contained two burial chambers, both orientated to the east. Only the west chamber retains its stone walls. During excavation, late quantities of flat stones with single-character Buddhist votive inscriptions dating from the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] were discovered.
The site is open to the public as an [[:en:Archaeological park|Archaeological park]], but the interior of the burial chambers are not accessible.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Onohara Kofun Group (Kan'onji)]][[en:Ōnohara Kofun cluster]][[ja:大野原古墳群 (観音寺市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q105070840]] Wikidata name: Onohara Kofun Group (Kan'onji) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Kofun period Kofun cluster in Kan'onji, Kagawa, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Ōnohara Kofun cluster | native_name =大野原古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Wankashizuka Kofun, entrance.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Wankashizuka Kofun entry | map_type = Japan Kagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ōnohara Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|05|16|N|133|39|48|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location =[[:en:Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji, Kagawa]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]; {{ill|Kan'onji|WD=Q861270}}| region = [[Shikoku]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built =late 6th - mid-7th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|05|16|N|133|39|48|E|}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Ōnohara Kofun cluster'''|大野原古墳群|Ōnohara Kofun-gun|}} is a group of ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]]s located in the city of [[:en:Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]], on the island of [[Shikoku]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumuli were collectively designated a National Historic Site in 2015 with the area under protection expanded in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003882|title=大野原古墳群|trans-title= Ōnohara Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
==Overview== The Ōnohara Kofun cluster is located in the western part of Kagawa Prefecture, in the center of an [[:en:Alluvial fan|Alluvial fan]] formed by the Kushita River, overlooking the [[Seto Inland Sea]]. The site once consisted of over 170 tumuli, but most have been destroyed through urban encroachment and farmland improvement, and only the four largest tumuli have survived under National Historic Site protection. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been carried out since 2006. The Wankashizuka Kofun, Hirazuka Kofun and Iwakurazuka Kofun are large circular Kofuns ( {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}), whereas the Kakuzuka Kofun is a large rectangular Kofun ({{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}). All have large stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s measuring 10 or more meters in length. In particular, the Wankashizuka Kofun has a total length of 14.8 meters and floor area of 24.6 square meters, or 72.7 cubic meters, making it the largest in Shikoku. From artifacts and [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] recovered, these tumuli are presumed to have been constructed from the late Kofun period from the end of the 6th century to the early 7th century. In the Shikoku region, there are many examples of individual tumuli of a class similar to the ones in this group, but the Ōnohara Kofun cluster is unique in that several large tumuli were built in a group over three generations for about 50 years.
The '''Wankashizuka Kofun''' is located behind the main hall of Onohara Hachiman Shrine. The tumulus is circular, measuring 37.2 meters in diameter and 9.5 meters in height, and was surrounded by a double moat, with a small embankment in between the moats. The burial chamber is a multi-room structure, consisting of a ear chamber, an antechamber, and a passageway, and opens to the southwest. It has a total length of 14.8 meters, with the rear chamber measuring 6.8 meters long, 3.6 meters widest, currently 3.9 meters high. Among the excavated items are pieces of [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery and fragments of a wooden coffin. These artifacts date the tumulus to the end of the 6th century.
The '''Hirazuka Kofun''' has a chapel of the Onohara Hachiman Shrine on its summit. The tumulus is circular, and with a diameter of 50.2 meters is the largest tumulus in Kagawa Prefecture. It is surrounded by an 8.4-meter wide moat. The burial chamber opens in the south-southwest direction and is a single-room structure with a total length of 13.2 meters. The rear chamber is 6.5 meters long, 3 meters wide at maximum, and 2.6 meters high and was found to contain Sure ware [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and other items which date it to the beginning of the 7th century.
The '''Kakuzuka Kofun''' is a square tumulus located adjacent to Onohara Central Park. It measures 41.7 x 37.8 meters, making it then largest square tumuli in Kagawa Prefecture. It was surrounded by a moat with a width of approximately 3.3 meters. The burial chamber opened to the south-southeast and has a length of 12.5 meters, with an inner chamber measuring 4.7 meters long, 2.6 meters widest, and 2.4 meters high. The stone used was mostly granite. Inside, fragments of Sue pottery pottery were found, which were dated to around the first half of the 7th century
The '''Iwakurazuka Kofun''' is located in the precincts of Onohara Hachiman Shrine, close to the Wankarizuka Kofun, and excavations revealed that it was built over a portion of the outer moat of that tumulus. The Iwakurazuka Kofun is elliptical, with a major axis of 38.2 meters and minor axis of 36.4 meters and contained two burial chambers, both orientated to the east. Only the west chamber retains its stone walls. During excavation, late quantities of flat stones with single-character Buddhist votive inscriptions dating from the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] were discovered.
The site is open to the public as an [[:en:Archaeological park|Archaeological park]], but the interior of the burial chambers are not accessible.

In the Shikoku region, there are many examples of individual tumuli of a class similar to the ones in this group, but the Ōnohara Kofun cluster is unique in that several large tumuli were built in a group over three generations for about 50 years.
The '''Wankashizuka Kofun''' is located behind the main hall of Onohara Hachiman Shrine. The tumulus is circular, measuring 37.2 meters in diameter and 9.5 meters in height, and was surrounded by a double moat, with a small embankment in between the moats. The burial chamber is a multi-room structure, consisting of a ear chamber, an antechamber, and a passageway, and opens to the southwest. It has a total length of 14.8 meters, with the rear chamber measuring 6.8 meters long, 3.6 meters widest, currently 3.9 meters high. Among the excavated items are pieces of [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery and fragments of a wooden coffin. These artifacts date the tumulus to the end of the 6th century.
The '''Hirazuka Kofun''' has a chapel of the Onohara Hachiman Shrine on its summit. The tumulus is circular, and with a diameter of 50.2 meters is the largest tumulus in Kagawa Prefecture. It is surrounded by an 8.4-meter wide moat. The burial chamber opens in the south-southwest direction and is a single-room structure with a total length of 13.2 meters. The rear chamber is 6.5 meters long, 3 meters wide at maximum, and 2.6 meters high and was found to contain Sure ware [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and other items which date it to the beginning of the 7th century.
The '''Kakuzuka Kofun''' is a square tumulus located adjacent to Onohara Central Park. It measures 41.7 x 37.8 meters, making it then largest square tumuli in Kagawa Prefecture. It was surrounded by a moat with a width of approximately 3.3 meters. The burial chamber opened to the south-southeast and has a length of 12.5 meters, with an inner chamber measuring 4.7 meters long, 2.6 meters widest, and 2.4 meters high. The stone used was mostly granite. Inside, fragments of Sue pottery pottery were found, which were dated to around the first half of the 7th century
The '''Iwakurazuka Kofun''' is located in the precincts of Onohara Hachiman Shrine, close to the Wankarizuka Kofun, and excavations revealed that it was built over a portion of the outer moat of that tumulus. The Iwakurazuka Kofun is elliptical, with a major axis of 38.2 meters and minor axis of 36.4 meters and contained two burial chambers, both orientated to the east. Only the west chamber retains its stone walls. During excavation, late quantities of flat stones with single-character Buddhist votive inscriptions dating from the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] were discovered.
The site is open to the public as an [[:en:Archaeological park|Archaeological park]], but the interior of the burial chambers are not accessible.

The {{nihongo|'''Ōnohara Kofun cluster'''|大野原古墳群|Ōnohara Kofun-gun|}} is a group of ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]]s located in the city of [[:en:Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]], on the island of [[Shikoku]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumuli were collectively designated a National Historic Site in 2015 with the area under protection expanded in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003882|title=大野原古墳群|trans-title= Ōnohara Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}}</ref>
